activation_policy.behavior_rule: Only approved lessons may change agent behavior. Candidate lessons may be shown in reports as pending learning material.

source_materials.title: 巴菲特定性分析
source_materials.relevance: Business quality, moat, management, circle of competence

source_materials.title: 巴菲特定量分析
source_materials.relevance: Financial metrics, ROIC controversies, gross margin interpretation

source_materials.title: 巴菲特财务报表分析方法论
source_materials.relevance: Owner earnings, ROIC, valuation caveats, accounting-to-economic bridge

source_materials.title: Value Investor Business-Model Research Playbook
source_materials.relevance: Business-model research workflow, unit economics, moat durability, source hierarchy, primary research, scenario modeling

source_materials.title: Gemini 商业模式尽职调查框架
source_materials.relevance: Scuttlebutt, fieldwork, industry clusters, scale economies shared, expert networks, channel checks, compliance controls

source_discovery.lesson: Use official filings, company IR, and official exchange disclosures as primary sources for financial numbers.
source_discovery.control_note: Unofficial sources can support qualitative research but cannot override official financial statements.

source_discovery.lesson: For Chinese and ADR companies, preserve listing, holding-company, operating-company, VIE, ADR, and exchange context before interpreting financial data.
source_discovery.control_note: Identity ambiguity should be treated as a source-risk issue.

source_discovery.lesson: Use a strict source hierarchy: official filings and regulator/exchange sources first, company-controlled public surfaces second, official public statistics third, then primary research, alternative data, and management follow-up.
source_discovery.control_note: Vendor, forum, and management/IR follow-up sources should accelerate or clarify research, not replace original-source work.

source_discovery.lesson: Distinguish desk research, channel checks, expert networks, scuttlebutt, and physical fieldwork as separate source types with different cost, information advantage, and compliance risk.
source_discovery.control_note: Primary research sources should be used only for targeted questions and must carry source-quality and compliance labels.

financial_extraction.lesson: Treat accounting numbers as starting evidence, then preserve raw source tables and normalized facts separately.
financial_extraction.control_note: Do not overwrite raw facts with interpreted or adjusted facts.

financial_extraction.lesson: Flag accounting classification shifts, net-versus-gross revenue issues, one-time gains, subsidies, and investment income as possible quality-of-earnings issues.
financial_extraction.control_note: These flags require evidence from filings before they can become conclusions.

financial_extraction.lesson: Normalize company KPIs into metric definition, company method, investor relevance, historical series, and failure mode.
financial_extraction.control_note: KPI definition changes should be preserved as audit evidence because they can affect comparability and trust.

financial_verification.lesson: Material source conflicts should block confident conclusions until reconciled or explicitly accepted.
financial_verification.control_note: The current materiality threshold is more than 2%, with clear rounding differences accepted.

metrics.lesson: Owner earnings should focus on cash that can accrue to owners after real maintenance needs and shareholder dilution costs.
metrics.control_note: V1 uses OCF minus SBC minus D&A as a conservative placeholder until maintenance CapEx is better estimated.

metrics.lesson: ROIC is judgment-heavy and should disclose choices around cash, goodwill, R&D capitalization, tax rate, impairments, and beginning/average/ending invested capital.
metrics.control_note: V1 calculates ROIC without applying any hard threshold.

metrics.lesson: Gross margin durability can support moat analysis, but margin changes need context around pricing power, utilization, product mix, and accounting classification.
metrics.control_note: Gross margin should not be interpreted as moat evidence without business-model context.

metrics.lesson: Business-model metrics should explain revenue quality, unit economics, retention/cohort health, pricing power, margin structure, capital intensity, cash conversion, ROIC, working-capital advantages, moat indicators, governance, and accounting trust.
metrics.control_note: Metrics should be industry-relative and tied to source-linked assumptions, not universal thresholds.

business_model_moat.lesson: Start with whether the business is understandable and inside the investor's circle of competence.
business_model_moat.control_note: Fast-changing technology alone is not a rejection reason if the business model and customer value proposition remain stable.

business_model_moat.lesson: A seriously interesting company should show durable customer value, pricing power or other franchise traits, high returns on capital, and low incremental capital needs.
business_model_moat.control_note: Financial signals should support, not replace, direct business-model evidence.

business_model_moat.lesson: Reject or heavily penalize commodity-like businesses without differentiation, pricing power, durable moat, or predictable long-term demand.
business_model_moat.control_note: This is a candidate screening rule, not an automatic rejection until approved.

business_model_moat.lesson: Translate business-model narrative into an economic engine: who pays, why they pay, what it costs to acquire and serve them, why they stay, capital consumed, and owner cash generated through a cycle.
business_model_moat.control_note: The agent should not treat a better company description as sufficient business-model understanding.

business_model_moat.lesson: Evaluate business-model clarity, economic viability, durability, capital efficiency, governance quality, accounting trustworthiness, and valuation sufficiency as decision criteria.
business_model_moat.control_note: Each criterion needs source-linked evidence and should be industry-relative.

business_model_moat.lesson: Escalate research in ROI order: filing/accounting deep dive, unit economics, industry and competitor map, scenario/reverse DCF, governance, product tests, interviews, fieldwork, alternative data, then management follow-up.
business_model_moat.control_note: External research should target valuation-relevant unknowns left unresolved by filings and modeling.

business_model_moat.lesson: A business-model report should explicitly answer customer value proposition, unit economics, return protection, management capital allocation, and what could break the model quickly.
business_model_moat.control_note: If these cannot be answered with confidence, intrinsic value may be hard to estimate.

business_model_moat.lesson: Analyze moat in two layers: industry structure and company-specific value creation, including profit pools, entry barriers, substitution risk, value-chain position, cost advantage, intangible assets, and execution skill.
business_model_moat.control_note: The agent should not judge a company moat without first identifying whether the industry itself allows durable excess returns.

business_model_moat.lesson: Treat scale economies shared as a testable moat hypothesis: scale lowers unit cost, management reinvests savings into customer value, lower prices increase volume, and higher volume deepens the cost advantage.
business_model_moat.control_note: For PDD/Temu-like models, this is a hypothesis requiring customer, merchant, supplier, and competitor evidence before becoming a conclusion.

business_model_moat.lesson: Use desk research and modeling to identify precise knowledge gaps before deploying expensive fieldwork or expert calls.
business_model_moat.control_note: Fieldwork should start from model-sensitive hypotheses, not broad curiosity.

leadership_people.lesson: Evaluate whether management behaves like owner-partners through integrity, clarity, incentives, capital allocation, and per-share value creation.
leadership_people.control_note: Leadership judgments from interviews or media require source-quality labels.

leadership_people.lesson: Careless dilution, weak communication, or capital allocation that destroys per-share value should trigger leadership review.
leadership_people.control_note: Evidence should come from filings, share-count history, compensation disclosures, and management communications.

leadership_people.lesson: Proxy, governance, and capital-allocation review should test whether incentives reward per-share value creation, ROIC, cash flow, and rational reinvestment rather than raw size.
leadership_people.control_note: Management and IR follow-up should clarify narrow source-linked questions, not create the initial thesis.

leadership_people.lesson: When evaluating scale-economies-shared businesses, test whether management is truly willing to reinvest scale benefits into customer value rather than harvest short-term margin.
leadership_people.control_note: Evidence should come from pricing history, margin behavior, capital allocation language, and competitor/customer observations.

valuation.lesson: Valuation should avoid false precision and focus on a range of owner cash flows that are predictable enough to matter.
valuation.control_note: Important valuation assumptions require approval before they drive conclusions.

valuation.lesson: Enterprise value that subtracts all cash must flag that some cash may not be excess, freely distributable, or practically usable by owners.
valuation.control_note: V1 subtracts all cash but must show this limitation.

valuation.lesson: True yield and owner earnings yield should be compared with long-term bond yields only after business quality and cash-flow durability are understood.
valuation.control_note: No price conclusion should be produced without scenario assumptions.

valuation.lesson: Margin of safety should come after valuation and risk assessment; it should widen when unit economics, capital needs, or moat durability are more uncertain.
valuation.control_note: A fixed margin-of-safety rule should not substitute for scenario modeling and downside appraisal.

customer_happiness.lesson: Customer and partner reception can support moat analysis by revealing switching costs, trust, repeat usage, product quality, and brand strength.
customer_happiness.control_note: Social, forum, video, and review sources need source-quality labels and cannot be used as primary financial sources.

customer_happiness.lesson: Customer, supplier, distributor, former-employee, and competitor evidence should focus on behavior and economics: why customers buy, what alternatives exist, what causes switching, and who has bargaining power.
customer_happiness.control_note: Anecdotes should not become thesis-critical evidence unless triangulated across independent sources.

customer_happiness.lesson: Structure scuttlebutt around customers, suppliers/distributors/dealers, competitors, and former employees because each group reveals a different part of moat durability.
customer_happiness.control_note: Competitor criticism is noisy, but grudging competitor respect can be a meaningful moat signal when independently corroborated.

customer_happiness.lesson: For tangible or operationally complex businesses, product tests, store/site visits, factory checks, logistics-node checks, and industry-cluster visits can validate whether the business model works in physical reality.
customer_happiness.control_note: Field observations should be timestamped, source-linked, and mapped back to a specific hypothesis or valuation assumption.

competitor_comparison.lesson: Compare competitors only after each company has gone through a comparable workflow for sources, financials, business model, people, and valuation context.
competitor_comparison.control_note: Side-by-side metrics should disclose accounting-standard and reporting-segment differences.

competitor_comparison.lesson: Competitor research should map both industry structure and company-specific advantages, including whether rivals can copy the cost structure, product architecture, distribution, or operating culture.
competitor_comparison.control_note: Rival claims need bias labels and should be triangulated before being used as moat evidence.

report_builder.lesson: Reports should distinguish facts, calculations, interpretations, unresolved issues, and human-review gates.
report_builder.control_note: The final checklist should map to right business model, right people, and right price.

report_builder.lesson: Every major claim should be traceable to a source, evidence type, confidence score, failure mode, and valuation relevance.
report_builder.control_note: The main report should not bury the decision criteria; detailed source linkage belongs in the data-linkage appendix.

report_builder.lesson: Primary research, expert-network, channel-check, and alternative-data evidence should include compliance/MNPI risk flags and a record of whether the evidence is public, non-material, or requires human review.
report_builder.control_note: The system should not encourage use of confidential or material nonpublic information.
